claim: UNICEF, WHO, and World Bank joint estimates report 45 million children under five with wasting globally, 13.6 million with severe acute malnutrition, with SAM prevalence exceeding 5 percent in several Sub-Saharan African countries and treatment coverage below 30 percent region-wide.

source.title: Levels and Trends in Child Malnutrition - JME 2023

method: Reviewed JME report wasting prevalence estimates, regional breakdowns, and methodology documentation.
limitations: JME estimates are modeled at national level and may smooth district-level variation.
SAM treatment coverage data is not systematically collected by household surveys.

claim: The 2021 Global Nutrition Report found that only 6 of 51 Sub-Saharan African countries were on track to meet the 2025 World Health Assembly target for reducing childhood wasting, with conflict, climate shocks, and weak health systems identified as primary barriers.
method: Reviewed wasting chapter, country progress assessments, and barrier analysis.
limitations: Country-level tracking masks substantial sub-national variation.
Treatment coverage data depends on country reporting completeness.
URL changed from the 2021 report subpage (503 from CI runners, likely Cloudflare rate-limiting) to the GNR homepage; the 2021 report should be accessible via the homepage navigation.

claim: A 2022 systematic review found that community-based management of acute malnutrition achieves recovery rates above 90 percent when treatment coverage exceeds 70 percent, but median coverage across Sub-Saharan Africa was 27 percent, with the largest gaps in conflict-affected and remote rural districts.

source.title: Coverage of community-based management of severe acute malnutrition programmes in Sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review

method: Reviewed systematic search, coverage estimation methods, and gap analysis.
limitations: Coverage measurement methods vary substantially across studies.
Treatment quality, not just coverage, affects recovery rates.
